junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Sees 'fully funded' in an offer letter and treats financial security as confirmed; doesn't distinguish funding types or ask about year 4–6 continuity
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Asks explicitly what type of funding is offered (RA/TA/fellowship), what grant or mechanism funds it, what happens if that mechanism ends, and what funding looks like in years 4–6
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: RA funding tied to expiring grants can collapse mid-degree; TA positions become competitive in later years; students who don't model this face financial crises during dissertation completion

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Applies to a small number of programs to save time and fees, weighted toward reach schools based on ranking
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Constructs a balanced 8–12 program portfolio with 2–3 reach, 4–5 core, and 2–3 safety programs, with confirmed advisor interest in at least 5 programs before submitting
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Year-to-year admissions variability means even strong applicants face complete rejection with narrow portfolios; confirmed advisor interest dramatically increases conversion rates and reduces portfolio risk

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Applies for well-known fellowships like NSF GRFP without checking eligibility for international students
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Checks eligibility requirements for every fellowship before investing application effort, recognizing that major US fellowships vary significantly in international student eligibility
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: NSF GRFP is restricted to US citizens and permanent residents — international students who invest time in ineligible applications lose opportunity to identify and apply for fellowships they actually qualify for

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Builds application list by US News ranking, targets top-5 programs as primary strategy, treats lower-ranked programs as fallbacks with less investment
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Builds application list by faculty research fit and advisor quality, treats ranking as a secondary tiebreaker, invests equally in researching advisors at all target programs
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The admissions mechanism is faculty fit and bandwidth — a ranking-first strategy systematically misallocates effort and produces rejections from programs where the applicant had no advisor match

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Evaluates advisors by publication count, h-index, or lab prestige; assumes top programs house strong mentors; skips talking to current students
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Evaluates advisors by placement record, student authorship patterns, funding status, and direct accounts from current and former students — treats advising quality as independent of prestige
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Advisor relationship is the strongest predictor of PhD success; choosing based on prestige proxies instead of mentorship evidence leads to years of poor outcomes that ranking cannot compensate for

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Frames SOP as a personal narrative — opens with biographical motivation, lists accomplishments, conveys passion for the field
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Frames SOP as a research document — opens with a specific research problem, connects prior work to readiness, names target faculty and recent papers, explains program fit
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Committees screen for research readiness in 30–60 seconds; a narrative SOP fails the initial cut regardless of the strength of the underlying profile

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Either avoids faculty contact (thinking it's presumptuous) or sends generic mass emails to maximize reach
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Sends targeted, personalized emails to 3–6 carefully selected faculty per program cycle, demonstrates knowledge of specific recent work, treats positive responses as primary application assets
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Faculty advocates inside programs are the mechanism that turns competitive files into admissions; generic outreach either produces no response or a negative impression

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Pursues the most famous or highest-status letter writers available, regardless of how well they know the student's research work
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Selects writers who directly supervised research and can provide specific evidence of capability; accepts less prominent writers if they know the work closely
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Committees read letters as peer assessments of research ability — specificity and credibility are what they extract; famous names without substance don't move decisions

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Applies to 3–5 programs, concentrates on top targets, assumes quality of application will carry the outcome in any given year
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Applies to 8–12 programs in a tiered portfolio (reach / realistic / safety), ensures 3+ potential advisors per program, accounts for year-to-year variance in faculty bandwidth and funding
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: A single bad cycle — key faculty on leave, unfunded, or overcommitted — can collapse a small portfolio entirely; diversification is the only protection against structural variance

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Reading 'full independence' as an advisor signal
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Interprets advisor offers of full autonomy as a positive — assumes independence means respect and trust
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Treats 'very independent' student descriptions as a warning sign of the Ghost archetype; probes specifically for meeting frequency, responsiveness, and whether the advisor invests in students' career outcomes
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Ghost advisors produce delayed degrees, weak publication records, and poor job market positioning — students who don't detect the pattern early pay for it across the entire PhD

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Uses overall program ranking as the primary filter — builds application list by ranking order and treats top-10 as universally superior to top-30
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Evaluates programs at the advisor level first — identifies specific faculty whose research aligns, then considers funding security, placement data for their specific career track, and location relative to target employers
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Rankings are aggregate metrics that obscure advisor-specific quality — the advisor relationship is the primary determinant of PhD outcome, and a junior evaluator using rankings as a proxy misses the most predictive variable

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Assumes a PhD offer is a PhD offer — does not distinguish between institutionally guaranteed multi-year funding and advisor-contingent year-by-year funding
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Reads offer letters carefully for funding guarantee language, asks about grant contingency directly, and treats funding security as a primary acceptance criterion alongside research fit
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Accepting a PhD with contingent funding and then losing stipend mid-program is a catastrophic outcome — especially for international students who cannot bridge the gap through federal aid or casual employment

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: What counts as due diligence before accepting
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Accepts an offer based on program reputation and official materials without speaking to current students or the advisor about real working conditions
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Treats conversations with current PhD students in the lab and a substantive discussion with the potential advisor as non-negotiable steps before accepting any offer — knows that official materials are marketing
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Advising style mismatches, dysfunctional lab cultures, and inadequate departmental support are invisible in official materials but consistently visible to current students — missing this information means deciding blind on a 5–6 year commitment

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How the MS vs. PhD decision is framed
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Frames the MS as the safer, faster, more accessible path — focuses on time-to-degree without fully modeling the financial cost difference or comparing against funded PhD alternatives
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Treats the MS vs. PhD decision as an explicit financial and career ROI analysis — calculates total MS cost including tuition and living expenses, compares against funded PhD stipend and credential value, and assesses immigration pathway differences
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: The unfunded MS can cost $150,000–$200,000 with uncertain employment outcomes while the funded PhD pays a stipend — a student who doesn't model this difference may make a financially catastrophic choice while believing they are being conservative

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How prestige is used as a signal
junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats prestige as a universal good that transfers across career tracks — assumes a top-5 program produces better outcomes than a top-30 program regardless of career goal
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Uses prestige selectively — weights it heavily for academic hiring and elite firm pipelines where pedigree filtering is documented, and discounts it for most industry careers where skills and portfolio dominate
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Chasing prestige in a career context where it doesn't transfer leads to poor program fit, wrong alumni networks, and misaligned advising — with no benefit to offset the costs

junior_vs_senior_signals.junior_pattern: Treats location as a lifestyle preference or cost-of-living factor — secondary to academic reputation in the decision
junior_vs_senior_signals.senior_pattern: Treats location as a structural career variable — explicitly maps program location against target employer geography and weights proximity to industry hubs as a concrete job search advantage, especially for OPT-constrained international students
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: For international students with a hard OPT deadline, being geographically distant from target employers during the most critical job search period is a measurable career disadvantage that no amount of program prestige fully offsets
